title: Paint a Line: The First Loop
lesson:
Storing 32 pixels with 32 separate STA instructions would be miserable. This is exactly the problem index registers solve. STA $0200,X stores to address $0200 plus X, so if you increment X and repeat, you sweep right across memory.

The loop is the heart of all programming, and on the 6502 it looks like this: do the work, INX (increment X), compare X to a limit with CPX, and BNE (Branch if Not Equal) back to the top. CPX sets the Zero flag when X equals the value, and BNE keeps looping while it's clear. It's the same loop you'll write in EE 306, just a little closer to the metal.
prompt: Fill the whole top row of the screen, all 32 pixels from $0200 to $021F, with any non-black colour, using a loop.
starterCode:
; Fill 32 pixels, using X as the index.
LDX #$00       ; X = 0
LDA #$08       ; burnt orange
loop:
  STA $0200,X  ; screen[$0200 + X] = A
  INX          ; X = X + 1
  CPX #$20     ; compare X with 32
  BNE loop     ; loop while X is not 32
BRK
hints: The top row is 32 pixels wide, and 32 is $20 in hex.
STA $0200,X writes to $0200 plus X. Increment X each pass with INX.
Stop when X reaches $20: CPX #$20 then BNE loop.
successText: Every pixel from $0200 to $021F must be painted (non-black).
reference:
LDX #$00
LDA #$08
loop:
  STA $0200,X
  INX
  CPX #$20
  BNE loop
BRK
